Thanks for this detailed bug report - and sorry for the long delay in reacting to it. I will change the digest handler to ignore auth-int rather than pretend to use it without actually incoporating the body into the digest. There's a number of reasons _not_ to support auth-int, and we should stop pretending that we do.

Sure. Just the other week I was browsing and I see the situations are well-defined, but making the server module I was just following the RFC. If only it were as full-featured as most cookie session controllers :) Thanks for the help.

Marking this fixed for the case described. auth-int is simply ignored. If auth-int is the only qop advertised by server, the digest handler will fall back to RFC 2069 behavior. If auth and auth-int qop values are advertised, auth qop will be chosen.

: There are several quirks with Chrome's HTTP digest authentication (specified in RFC 2617) that seem noncompliant, though none of them too pressing. They are valid combinations which the RFC say Chrome should accept, and it does, but behaves oddly. To reproduce two of them I've found: 1. Connect to a server which sends a digest challenge qop="auth-int" 2. Chome will send back a response containing qop=auth-int, but where the second hash component does not include the hashed entity body, as is required in this case. 1. Connect to a server which sends a digest challenge qop="auth,auth-int" 2. Chrome will send back qop=, but use the variant of the response hash with a nonce count, client nonce, and (empty string) qop, which requires a response of either qop=auth or qop=auth-int. I've attached a text file containing the authentication headers, obtained from a Wireshark capture of the loopback interface, with analyses of Chrome's response. The server is Happstack, a Haskell framework, and I was writing a module to do digest authentication when I came across these behaviors. As auth-int isn't too commonly used, this shouldn't affect too many client/server interactions in the wild. Nonetheless, Firefox seems to handle the requests fine: if given auth-int, it ignores it, falling back to the RFC 2069 standard if nothing else is specified, which is permitted by RFC 2617.
45194: Incorrect digest authentication behavior with less common qop values

Potentially related (please let me know if I should open a new bug for this, or if this is somehow expected behavior): STR: 1) Visit -- red skull and crossbones are shown (nice job, Discover Bank) 2) Now visit in the same tab -- red skull and crossbones remain 3) Visit in a new tab -- all indicators are green If the red skull and crossbones indicator is to be useful and trustworthy, it should only appear when there is a threat. I am not aware of any reason why visiting the same page would be a threat in one tab/process, and not in another.
Insecure script pollutes the hostname across the entire process because an attacker can use it to affect the display of pages from that host across the entire process. This is working as intended.
In step 2, you get the skull and crossbones even if you paste that URL into the omnibox, which I believe ought to create a new process. Dr. Barth? Dr. Reis?

I would assume it doesn't create a new process for speed reasons (as if you're navigating on the same site, we'd expect it to use similar resources, and thus the cache for this renderer already has the right contents), but I don't know.

Re: comment 5 , Peter's right. The URLs for step 1 and step 2 are from the same site, so we don't create a new process when you navigate. That means it keeps the red skull and crossbones indicator. The threat in those steps is that after step 1, the attacker injects code into some other page from discoverbank.com in the same process (e.g., a new popup window), which then injects code into the login page after step 2.

I'm going to call this "working as intended". The only part of that that's questionable is whether we should try to create a new process when the user types an address, and I think the performance penalty there outweighs the benefit of not displaying a skull in the narrow case of "I was on a site with bad HTTPS and typed in a new address on the same site that would have good HTTPS".

The case that we're not getting correct is as follows: 1) There's only one tab in a the process. 2) We're navigating the main frame. In that case, there's nowhere for the attacker's sneaky JavaScript to hide, so we can clear out the "this origin might be infected" state in that process. This might be easy to fix now that Peter had made some infrastructure improvements to the SSLManager. I'll try to get a working Chromium build and check it out.

A user with a new computer who installs Chrome will get the set of random default bookmarks that the OEM put in the default browser. Chrome should only import bookmarks that people actually want. We tried detecting the set of default bookmarks by looking at their creation time, but that failed in cases where people had performed a backup. Urs suggested building some kind of classifier that takes a fingerprint of the imported bookmarks and checks it to make sure it's not too common a set. If it is, either prompt the user, or ignore those bookmarks. We'd need to augment this if we wanted to handle the case where there are default bookmarks mixed with some user-initiated bookmarks.

Even worse: on open networks, an attacker could MitM inject a script into whatever HTTP page the user requests, this script could then open any number of new windows or iframes with known HTTP pages that ask for cc data that the user may have visited. The attacker could then again MitM inject some more script into each of those pages and steal any auto-fill data that the user saved. No user interaction required other than the victim visiting any HTTP page over an unencrypted network. If we can detect cc data being entered over HTTP, we probably want to warn the user as well as refuse auto-fill. Otherwise, the user may think Chrome's auto-fill is broken rather than realize the risk s/he's in.

Hmmm, interesting. That is concerning. Submitting credit card info over HTTP is always a bad idea anyway, so I think it would be good for Chrome to warn the user and prevent them from doing this. That being said, I'm not sure if that safeguard should be built into the AutoFill feature, or just Chrome itself. What if we did this: If the user selects an AutoFill profile which contains a credit card number *AND* we detect that the page is non-HTTPS, then don't autofill anything, and we display a message which explains to the user that this is dangerous. This shouldn't happen a lot for most internet users since all legitimate shopping sites use HTTPS, so I'm not too concerned about the exact UI (infobar or pop-up dialog is fine, I guess). Security folks, is there any existing collateral we can point to which explains HTTP vs. HTTPS for laymen?

just to be clear, the infobar would not be a Yes/No decision. It would simply be a way to tell the user that AutoFill is intentionally disabled because it's a non-HTTPS page. I haven't looked at just what is failing just yet, but I am observing that the ShmPixmap is being frequently recreated (seemingly about once per update). This is causing the driver to do a lot of unnecessary work binding (and unbinding upon the destroy) the pages into the GPU address space.

Is this the most recent version: yes OS + version: MeeGo 1.0 CPU architecture (32-bit / 64-bit): 32-bit Window manager: Mutter URLs (if relevant): Behavior in Linux Firefox: Behavior in Windows Chrome (if you have access to it): 1. Open the page " " using Chromium browser; 2. Check CPU utilization of the browser (including renderer and plugin) and X processes, e.g., using Top command; 3. browser processes (browser + renderer + plugin) consume ~85% CPU and X server consumes If SHM pixmaps support is available, for example, Intel drivers now support that ( ), and if Chromium browser can use this support in windowless plugin painting, the CPU utilization can be improved a lot (e.g. with the proposed change which will be attached later, the browser processes consume ~60% CPU and X server consumes ~20% CPU when browsing the same page). Chromium browser and X server consume too much CPU in Windowless plugin painting, and from our sampling result it's mainly caused by memory copying between the Ximages and pixmaps for GetImage/PutImage requests. For example, before windowless plugin painting the plugin delegate always copies the image buffer (shared between renderer and plugin) into an X server pixmap, and after painting the plugin delegate copies the rendered image pixmap back into the image buffer. Attach a screenshot and backtrace if possible. Attached are the Top outputs for current Chromium browser and the modified Chromium browser w/ our changes to use SHM pixmaps support. Also the proposed code modification is attached, which modifies WindowlessPaint to directly use Xlib interfaces for SHM pixmaps support, similarly to the way how backing_store_x handles different SHM support levels provided by X server.

Robert Hansen's recent BlackHat talk noted a relatively minor leak due to Chrome's autosuggest functionality. It was not widely picked up -- perhaps due to being buried amongst lots of other https info during the talk. It turns out that if you type the following into the URL bar: Then autosuggest queries will be issued over plain http up to and including the '@' character. This obviously leaks both username and password. Normally, autosuggest for https:// stops at the end of the hostname to protect path and URL parameters (hostname is fundamentally leaked due to DNS). We could trivially stop at the : to avoid leaking the password. Ideally, we might never leak the username either. Best I can come up with is to delay requesting suggestions until we see a . in the hostname, e.g.: 1) https:// - 2a) https://blah . - 2b) https://blah : - 2c) https://blah@ - Not sure if this solution is acceptable. I would like to be able to create a Chrome extension that: 1. Shows whether the current page was loaded over IPv4 or IPv6. 2. Lists the IP addresses of the current domain. But, as discussed here, it's not currently possible without relying on an external HTTP daemon or NPAPI plugin: One possible API would be something in chrome.tabs that shows you all the IP addresses associated with the base domain of the current page. It would also be nice to know which specific address was used to load the page. Naturally, this would be undefined for file:// and such. Another approach would be to provide a general-purpose DNS resolver, allowing you to look up the records on any domain.

Huh? It sounds like you aren't familiar with the way the pinch gesture works; all it is is a series of small magnifications. What you are suggesting would make it nearly impossible to zoom. It also sounds like you missed the part of the code where the accumulation is discarded each time a new gesture starts--if you make a tiny pinch, lift your fingers, make a tiny pinch, etc. nothing accumulates. Yes, which isn't necessarily a feature. I got positive feedback from gesture users when I did it this way in Camino. It's also not relevant to the described bug: "I constantly find myself hitting command-0 to reset my zoom level back to the default." If someone didn't want to zoom at all, the difference between one zoom and n zooms is essentially irrelevant. Making it only accidentally zoom once won't help. If the bug is just that we are more sensitive than Safari, we can just bump up kZoomStepValue. As the comment said, it's entirely based on me playing around for a few hours and seeing what felt right. ( Bug 34489 is somewhat related, except that people are complaining that they have the same problem in Safari there, so it's less about the actual threshold value.)

But not a series of _very_ small magnifications :-) Currently, if you start a pinch-zoom, wait 20 minutes, and then finish the zoom gesture during the next 40 minutes, we'd zoom in, which seems wrong to me. Anyway, it was just a suggestion. Yes, I pasted above (which hints at me not missing it).

Shouldn't an alignment issue cause a SIGBUS and not SIGSEGV?

I do recall crashes in pickle historically, and they were most often associated with (as I recall) double free problems, where access to a buffer was performed after the buffer was freed (a second time). Sadly the error had nothing to do with pickle code, and was (last time) associated with a class during reference counting but failing to use a base. The current alignment in pickle is (based on the referenced bug) 8 byte alignment. As a result, we should not have trouble on a 64 bit platform (I think). The TODO/bug referenced suggests that we should handle misalignments manually, rather than adding space/size to get the 8 byte alignment. As noted, I *think* the SIGSEGV would be caused by a pointer into neverland, rather than a misalignment anyway.

Yeah, alignment should go into SIGBUS (I was really asking :) ). From looking at the code, we have an alignment issue for any platform that enables alignment checks, because there is no precondition about the alignment of the buffer that contains the pickled data. It looks like we don't run yet on a system where that matters. Also, there is a bug in the Pickle constructor, because it reads from the buffer regardless of the provided data length. It assumes that the buffer is at least size_t bytes, without actually verifying that... it will trigger a dcheck in debug mode, after reading from possibly invalid memory :( But that doesn't look like the problem here. Everything looks fine... except that this comes from a build that doesn't have the fix for bug 53910 , and that may cause this behavior. But that doesn't explain why this crash seems to be x64 specific.

Historically, this class of bug was caused by memory corruption (double free). I think the last time we saw it there was a mistake in a reference counted instance, which was not a thread-safe ref-count. Sadly it was decref'd on two threads, allowing it to race to freedom. This in turn induced a double free, and allowed memory (such as a pickle buffer) to be reused, and effectively overwritten.

It always reads only last file in list. <input type="file" multiple / <script function var files = input.files; for(i in files){ files[i].name, files[i]); // This will print CORRECT file name. var reader = new FileReader(); reader.onload = function(evt){ //But this will give WRONG data. Always reads last file. } } } </script
Chrome 8.0.552.11 dev also affected

This looks like a bug in the code - not a chrome bug. JavaScript doesn't have lexical scoping which means the 'reader' variable overwrites the previous one on each loop iteration (even with 'var'). And FileReader is asynchronous which means the 'onload' might not execute immediately. Please test with: <input type="file" multiple / <script function var files = input.files; for(i in files){ files[i].name, files[i]); // This will print CORRECT file name. } } function processFile(file){ var reader = new FileReader(); reader.onload = function(evt){ //But this will give WRONG data. Let's not call it integer overflows until we actually prove that there are any :).

This will always be a null given that these just fall into default cases for the type and format enums. Instead of returning 0 these should set an error condition (invalid type, invalid format etc) Doing this will cause a number of unit tests to fail though so these will need to be refactored slightly. I am dropping the security flags as I don't see how this could cause corruption.

I'm not sure I see how your example code would trigger the issue. I'm also not sure I see the issue. unpack_alignment is guaranteed to be 1, 2, 4 or 8 so in the case of an unknown format or type we'll get a size of 0. We'll allocate a buffer of size 0 and call the service. The service will check the format and type (only the service can check the format and type). It will generate invalid enum. I'll see if I can write a test to make it crash. It would be good to see what your tests do.

@cdn: Thanks for verifying, I wasn't sure if any part of the code multiplied width/height for an allocation without multiplying with format/type too and since there were so many locations where the code crashed, I assumed the worst. @gman: My tests generate random but valid JavaScript, which my framework records in a log before sending it to Chrome. When Chrome crashes, it is restarted and the framework replays the log to try to reproduce the issue. If it reproduces, it continues to replay the log with chunks removed in an attempt to minimize the log to only those instructions needed to trigger the issue. From the minimized logs I extracted the example code. There really isn't anything out-of-the ordinary in them that could explain these crashes. I have only seen this crash on one machine and not on any VM: could the hardware or driver be a factor? Do you know if hardware acceleration is even available in VMWare machines? Is there a chance this is caused by some form of race-condition? That could explain why it doesn't always crash and why it causes crashes in various locations in the code.

Yes and no, in my case while I know I have to live with the old flash I would still appreciate warnings for updates of other plugins. It seems like a disabling all checks using the command line option is a sledge hammer to crack a nut, unless I'm miss-understanding something?

If you are already using a plugin that has known, in-the-wild security vulnerabilities, there's no significant security advantage to having warnings on other plugins. There are pre-packaged exploit kits that try a whole suite of vulnerabilities until one works; having one exploit work instead of two doesn't really make you safer. Adding special UI to provide marginal-if-any benefit to a 0.01% use case is very much against the design philosophy of Chromium.

You could make the same argument about opening a port on a firewall to get something to work, there are exploits out there that can take advantage of that one port but you still open just the one because that's all you need. You don't turn the entire firewall off which is effectively what your suggesting imo.

You're ignoring the second paragraph of my comment, which is the important one. The question is not whether or not there is any theoretical value to you personally to having extra control. The question is whether there is sufficient value to warrant us coding and maintaining it, and all users having the overhead of that extra option, even though almost nobody would use it. The answer, according to the core design philosophy of Chromium, is very clearly no. (Also, you are ignoring the numbers in your analogy; I bet if 99.99% of firewall users never needed to open any ports, most firewall software wouldn't have UI for managing individual open ports.) You should put your energy into trying to get Abode to address the issue that's preventing you from upgrading, since that's the real solution here.

You already have the perfect place about:plugins or even add an option to the existing command line it doesn't need be something your average user sees. With respect to the analogy I would say in fact its about those numbers if not even less that ever even manage their firewall, yet they do all have a gui for doing so. I would love it if Adobe would fix the core issue, believe me I've tried, but its not just about this one plugin. Its about the fact there are legitimate reasons why a user might not be able to update, be that a bug in said "up to date" version the corporate policy etc. I'm not trying to awkward I just think its an important option which goes hand in hand with this new functionality even if its for the minority, which one could argue is the same for all of the options already in about:plugins for example, hope you understand :)

Hmm. This is a really interesting use-case. How do we best keep users safe who have to browse around with an unsafe Flash plugin? I'd suggest that the right thing to do for these users is to: - Set plug-ins mode to "Block" - Run with The user can then authorize web sites on a per-trusted-site basis. Note also that "Block" mode is more usable in the latest v10 dev releases thanks to the right-click-menu option to run individual plug-ins.

That's going down the lines or windows UAC which frankly just pisses users off enough to either stop using it or to turn the security protection off if possible. Totally agree with trying to be as secure as possible but when there are issues which prevent the user from doing so for whatever reason then the option needs to be there to confirm the exception globally. At the end of the day all the user needs to know is that its out of date and that they should upgrade to be secure. For 99.99% of users that will be enough and they will update. For the other 0.01% who have a specific issue and are aware of risks, simply allowing them to acknowledge that is good enough. Forcing them to accept on every site is not the way to go as it would be way too intrusive.

@nebiyu: your issue sounds different. It's the intention that preview releases (e.g. v10.2 or v11.x of Flash) would not be considered out-of-date. Maybe that's a separate bug; let us know and we can look into why you got blocked.

This is not a bug, it works as intened. Actually the popup was granted by user geature, it's because in the attached "event_mm.html", the untrusted(custom) event was fired inside a user-initiated onMouseOver event. If you create untrusted event inside a user-initiated (trusted) event, but fire it out of the user-initiated event, WebKit will block that popup. Please see the attached Test already covers this situation. Close this bug as WontFix. Please feel free to reopen it if you have different thought. Thanks.

If the implementation is as expected, I believe our design is bad: the attached demo makes it impossible (AFAIK) to not get the popup even with popup blocker enabled. This effectively negates the popup-blocker. Our design allows a site to open a popup for *all* user gestures. This means the only way a user can prevent seeing a popup on a page is to not do anything, which is not very practically or productive. IMHO we should only allow popups if the user clicks on an element in the page, or presses a key while an element in the page is focussed/active.

Currently in WebKit, a event in js context can only be considered as user gesture when matching both following two conditions (1) inside a state caused by user-initiated events (mostly are user input events). (2) the type of current JS event is mouse down/up/click, key down/up/press, touch events or other accepted events (such The artificial event created by createEvent method should be called untrusted event instead of custom event according to DOM3 Event module spec. In event_mm.html, the untrusted click event is created in mouse-move event (mouse-over is synthesized from mouse-move which is initiated by user) and itself is click event, that is why it passed the gesture check. I agree with Skyline that current gesture check has bug. The question is how we should check it. (1) we cannot only check the trusted events for judging user gesture because click events is untrusted event according to DOM3 Event module spec, it's a synthesized event.(focusing the hyperlink element and pressing the 'Enter' or 'Space' key, a click event would be dispatched as the default action of the respective keydown event, in that case, root cause event is mouse/key down) (2) Looks like we should not check the artificial event created by createEvent for user gesture, but it could break user's business logic. Think about the following scenario, *I want to open multiple links with one click on the button "open all". Now I can use artificial click event to open multiple links in the click handler of a button "open all". If WebKit disallows checking artificial event, my code will be broken. @Adam, any comments?

When setting the initially, we should just ignore mouseover / mousemove / etc events. We should whitelist the kinds of embedder events that set the indicator's state. All that work should be done in the layer before generating DOM-level events. The DOM-level events and their types aren't really relevant for deciding whether this callstack was originated by a user gesture.

The weird thing about this report is that this particular error message means that we failed reading something from disk, at a point where we already checked that the relevant data should be stored. In other words, this is an unexpected condition that has two common causes: - A system crash that caused some files to be corrupt. - A hardware failure And of course, there is also the option of a regular bug that makes us believe that the files are corrupt beyond recovery, when there was no system crash. This is not an issue with Incognito. It was an issue, if a certificate presented in incognito mode is silently presented again in normal mode. Can somebody from the network team look into this?

jochen: I don't understand your comment 2 . What you described doesn't seem to be a problem. If a user reveals his identity in incognito mode by presenting a client certificate, it should be fine for a browser to present the client certificate silently in normal mode. I will mark this issue as a duplicate of either issue 30877 or issue 47129 after getting clarification on comment 2 .

We should not leak information about incognito mode to regular mode. So if you go into incognito mode, present a certificate, and then close the incognito mode again, there shouldn't be any traces left as to where you've been during incognito mode.

Most likely a dupe of Issue 30877 , but I haven't looked into it yet. The SSL Client auth cache is managed as part of the by means of the HttpNetworkSession. My understanding is that HttpNetworkSession is unique per profile. However, the SSL session cache is shared amongst all profiles, so if an SSL session was established via client-auth, it's very likely to re-use that session in another profile, hence the information leak. davidben@ had been working on this (see ), but I don't know the status of how complete it is, and it's currently NSS only. This is related to quirks mode. If we add <!DOCTYPE html selector value is set to lowercase in quirks mode. Instead of setting selector text to lower case, if possible probably we might have to consider case insensitivity handling for quirks mode during selector matching. if selector-

As noted in #11, we currently set the selector value to lowercase in quirks mode. The first solution we tried was to always store the input value in the selector object, and compare case-insensitively during selector matching.[1] But an optimization (RuleSet[2]) meant that the elements were rejected before the case-insensitive code could match them. It would have been a non-trivial task to make RuleSet case-insensitive without sacrificing performance. Another solution would be to keep two fields in the CSSSelector class: both the lowercased name (for matching) and the input name (for serialization). We can re-use the existing RareData structure[3] to store them, so that non-quirks code doesn't pay for the extra space. This appears to be the best solution so far. [1] [2] [3]

See in for the nacl build file that needs exceptions.

The larger issue here is that the way we use clang to build isn't supported by xcodebuild. xcodebuild loads spec files such as 4.2.xcspec and uses this to convert etc to compiler flags. This will probably map to -fno-cxx-exceptions in clang's case.
andersca tells me that clang's behavior matches gcc's again, so maybe I can just revert my workaround in r77032 !

For hazy reasons, clang changed -fno-exceptions to disable Objective-C exceptions as well recently. Since Cocoa throws sometimes, we have to keep these enabled. If we just add -fobjc-exceptions to cflags, we get a "unused flag" warning for all normal C++ files we build. This is probably good enough as a band-aid, but should be fixed eventually. Mark mentions that xcode honors so we should probably add -fno-cxx-exceptions to that and don't use -fno-exceptions with clang. One problem is nacl, which needs exceptions – they currently just add it back to OTHER_CFLAGS. Mark suggested to have a gyp variable that controls if exceptions should be set or not, which nacl would then set to "exceptions plz", and common.gypi would map that to the right flag. That sounds like the right solution medium-term.

In order to take advantage of cached program binaries in ANGLE, we would need to implement this extension. This is only worth doing if we go ahead with the idea in 78727. For ANGLE, the binary could be the concatenation of the vertex and pixel shader binaries. This might allow us to increase the shader optimization level in ANGLE to full. I had to reduce it to minimal optimization level to get acceptable compilation times for complex shaders. We would still need to be careful not to have the compiler take a long time the first time a shader is encountered. Perhaps we could have ANGLE continue to use minimal optimization for glLinkProgram. We could have the GPU process itself (outside ANGLE) fire off the D3DCompile call on a background thread with full optimization enabled and then subsequently use glProgramBinary instead of glLinkProgram if the compiled binary is available.

Applications that do background image processing currently suffer from being unable to shift this work to a web worker. For example, an animated image carousel that downloads large images from the web and then processes them for display. If the image processing takes longer than a single frame and happens in the main thread, it's going to cause framerate hitches. Doing the processing in a worker would let the main thread maintain smooth framerate with the minimum amount of developer work (alternative is to roll your own co-operative multitasking scheduler and that's a pain.) Having the ability to create canvas elements and rendering contexts and share their results with the main thread (pass back a shared texture IDs or something) would help a lot in the above case.

This happens with chrome buildbot page kept running for several days, but could happen elsewhere, quickly. 1. Load chrome buildbot page. 2. Keep it running for several days. 3. Renderer locks up for several seconds. This is due to writing item with large number of subitems, This exposes a pathological case for Pickle, when it incrementally allocates just the size it needs for ever growing buffer. Imagine, extending 30MB buffer by 4 bytes to write next string and so on. However, with chrome versions 94878 and 95078 it no longer waits for the debugger to connect on port 4014 and no longer breaks on the entry point. I was browsing through source code in chrome, in particular tracing the kEnableNaclDebug variable that is set from It used to be in nacl_main.cc (in Chrome) that there was code that did this: if { ChildProcess nacl_process; bool debug = } This call to HasSwitch with kEnableNaClDebug was the only place in chrome that checked for the debug switch. In the latest version of Chrome, the equivalent location does this: if { NaClListener listener; listener.Listen(); } and when I do a 'Find in Files' in the Chrome solution (in Visual Studio on Windows) there is no longer a place found in the chrome code based that calls HasSwitch with kEnableNaclDebug, but the switch is still set by the command-line parser. It looks like the debug stuff got turned off with this CL: Because of issues with pyauto on integration bots, we don't have tests that flagged this... Please assign to mmortensen -- I'm working on a fix.

Similar problem with gl.

To elaborate on #1: The gl_bindings are a level of indirection that allows use to switch out the OpenGL implementation at runtime. Different implementations are: - Open GL ES - Desktop GL (GLX on Linux, WGL on Windows) - Angle (translation to D3D on Windows) - OSMesa (software rasterizer) It works by loading a bunch of function pointers mapping to actual GL functions on initialization. Code is unaware of the function pointers because they are called by macros. The browser compositor (defined in ui/gfx/compositor) uses the gl_bindings. This compositor is part of the views component. The browser itself use the gl_bindings in to get some info about the GPU that Chrome is running on. Both of these will have their own copies of the binding. This is wasteful, but to my knowledge no error is caused.

views (a component) depends on compositor (a static library) browser (a static library) depends on compositor (a static library) So both of them get code that creates a Singleton. Hence we get multiple Singletons on the build.

This is worthwhile if it doesn't significantly complicate the filtering code. If it's much more complex (or if not enough platforms allow the distinction to be made), then the existing solution is probably fine.

I think the cross-platform filtering code could stay the same. All that's needed is for the per-platform to only call host_- That said, I don't think we can do this with XRecord. Well, maybe we could, by selecting on DeviceMotionNotify instead of MotionNotify, but that relies on XInput support.

In Chromoting Host, the class listens for keyboard/mouse input events (using the XRecord extension, or XInput if Issue 97178 is implemented). This is used to implement floor-control (disabling injection if local devices are being used). However, makes no attempt to distinguish between events caused by a real device, and echoes of previously-injected events. Only a real device (not injection echoes) should cause floor-control to be activated. Currently, we try to detect echoes by storing a list of the 50 most recently injected mouse events. If an input matches any coordinates from this list, we assume it is an echo, rather than coming from a real local device. We should refine to do the detection, and only pass genuine events to the floor-control routine. The current logic could be kept as a fallback if detection can't be done directly. This can almost certainly be done using XInput. It may be possible (I haven't checked) with the current XRecord-based implementation.
97182: Simplify Chromoting floor-control on systems that can detect whether input events are real or injected.

OS Version: 6.1 (Windows 7, Windows Server 2008 R2) : None. (Only WebKit browsers need the workaround described below) 1. Load Wiki.png should be loaded once. Wiki.png is loaded twice. Attach a screenshot if possible. In WebKit browsers, Google's Maps API loads images via a hidden iframe so that in-flight image requests may be canceled. This is useful for example when a user repeatedly zooms: canceling in-flight requests for images at the previous zoom level allows to load the next zoom level faster. See linked code for example. Note that this is not necessary in Firefox or IE, in which changing the .src of an image cancels any in-flight requests - this is a workaround for the problem that WebKit browsers do not cancel in-flight image requests when image.src changes. Recently we noticed that images are fetched twice when loaded by this method, first without a referer header, second with a referer header. Double-fetching images is harmful to performance. I think the right solution here is #1: keep synchronous mode operation in MockHostResolver, but avoid it from having side effects. Here is why I think that: * #3 seems like the wrong fix since it moves unnecessary complexity into client socket, and still leaves us susceptible to the bug in other areas of code. It could easily regress in future changes. * #2 is not great either since having synchronous operation for the host resolver is actually fairly important for tests. We have had historical bugs in not properly handling synchronous failures/successes, which would manifest in the field in particular when pulling entries out of DNS cache. For instance we had something like this that glitched the client socket pool code. Some tests now rely on using synchronous mode operation to exercise these paths. I think it would be a mistake to remove that and only test everything with async DNS completions. I think your work in progress in is moving in the right direction. I just want to see it duplicate more of the logic of HostResolverImpl -- in particular, it should set entries in the cache in addition to looking them up in the cache.
szym is taking an alternate approach in - Reimplement so it doesn't use HostResolverImpl and in turn won't run a message loop.

This is more a bug to me, from a design standpoint. Listening to the Gerrit ref validation was able to catch only the externally refs but not the internal ones, like the

The current implementation of the multi-site plugin listens to all events generated by Gerrit in order to perform certain validations against a shared database. Unfortunately, the was initially designed to only validate refs created by the users and not the Gerrit internal ones. This means that all magic references generated internally are not handled by the multi-site and this is a requirement for fixing the split-brain problem that now exists in the plugin. The new suggested approach should instead capture *ALL* the reference updates that happen at the JGit level by extending the RefUpdates abstract class without requiring a change to the Gerrit code base.
Escalating to a P1, because the multi-site implementation it would risk to go into a split-brain condition

i've posted rework CLs for everything except actually setting the User-Agent header :) the way it works today is: - if `repo` is making the HTTP connection itself from its own Python code, it sets User-Agent to the custom one: (Linux) Python/2.7.16 - if repo is calling `git` which makes the HTTP connection, repo lets `git` set its own User-Agent header which will only contain the git version: git/2.22.0 the request as stated in comment #0 is for repo to override git's User-Agent setting with repo's extended one. i don't think we should do this exactly as the server will no longer have a way of differentiating these two scenarios: is it repo itself making the connection, or is it git simply being executed by repo ? after all, if we follow the UA [0] spec, the product is *not* git-repo in the latter case. what if we use the form: (Linux) would that satisfy the request ? that would make it clear that it's `git` being run by repo vs repo itself making the connection (which would continue to use the UserAgent defined today). The server supports automatic tracing of requests that fail with an 500 Internal Server Error [1]. This means if a request fails and the cause is non-recoverable, the server retries the request once with tracing enabled. This way we automatically capture a trace of the failure which makes the investigation of the issue much easier. With [2] (which should reach prd in Google this week) the ID of the trace is returned to the client. This is done by setting the "X-Gerrit-Trace" header in the HTTP response. If a request fails with 500 Internal Server Error the frontend should check for the "X-Gerrit-Trace" header in the HTTP response and display its value as trace ID in the error popup. The user should be told to include that trace ID into bug reports. [1] [2]
11521: Display trace ID in error popup if request failed and server did a trace

How much does Gerrit store in the session server side actually? I'm asking, because if this is amount of data is very small and not frequently updated, perhaps we could consider storing that on the client side (e.g. cookies), encrypted and signed by a server secret and make the session store stateless for Gerrit servers. (This is a wild thought, I'm not really knowledgeable how feasible that would be for Gerrit.)

That is defined on the cache settings for the sessions (web_sessions cache). The session is purely a number and it is already set as a cookie on the client side. What Gerrit keeps track on the server side is just two things: the session is still valid or not; what is the associated random account session key. We could actually have indeed something like a JWT token ( https://jwt.io/ ) so that it can be trusted and just stored on the client side. There will still be the same problem though related to session invalidation: you need to have a way to tell everyone that one specific session must be invalidated prematurely. In a nutshell, yes, I believe it is a good idea. However, I believe that the problem of propagating it to the other nodes would stay.

This is a feature request for a new command to be added to the Download UI's "REPO" tab. A very standard workflow is to download a change from Gerrit then want to make edits and then repo upload the changes back to Gerrit. However, there is a stumbling block in the existing UI which is that in order to perform a repo upload, you must have a local branch (that was created using repo start). However, for developers coming from the rest of the world (not Google) who are not familiar with the idiosyncrasies of repo, it is cumbersome to have to remember to create a new repo branch. In other words, you can't use your knowledge of git basics to get by with repo, and this download UI. So, to ease the development process, I suggest adding another item in this UI titled "Branch" that would have commands to both cherry-pick the change, as well as start a proper "repo" branch. This would lessen the amount of human toil in this workflow. $ repo download -c "apps/libapps" 1823178/6 && repo start --head cl-1823178 Note that it should probably come up with a unique but generic branch name, perhaps "cl-${Gerrit CL ID}", etc...

Please find attached a mock with the following proposition: - For changes below the max items threshold for each section, we write (N changes). - As we cannot know the exact number of all changes in section, we write for example (25 changes and more). The intent of stating "and more" is to let user know that there are more items than the ones presented under the section in the dashboard. In the current UI, the affordance to see all changes under a section in the dashboard is to hover on the section title which underlines the title (making it a link). When clicking on the section title, users are directed to another pages where all changes are listed. We'll keep this behavior at the moment.

What is the rationale behind being able to click "Has draft comments" if the total of changes is behind threshold of display (of 25 changes per section for example)? I have another option to propose which will include a bit more changes to the UI. 1. For changes below max changes threshold for each section (max 25 changes): At the moment, if users click on "Has comments section", users will access another page with the changes listed and users will see exactly the same information as wha they see in the dashboard for this section, which doesn't add much value. I propose we could remove the link to access a page in the case where Changes under a section is below N threshold. We display the number of changes in a lighter grey than the section title for maintaining more visual priority to the section title. 2. For section with "25 changes and more" In this case, users might want to access the page where the 25 changes and all the other changes that do not fit in the dashboard are listed. I propose we could underline "25 changes and more" by default as a link to signal that this is a link to access the page where all changes are listed (in the example, related to Ongoing reviews) . What do you think?

Frequently tech leads have a large number of CLs in their review queue but only a small fraction are actively awaiting a response from the reviewer. As a reviewer it can currently be very time consuming to find which subset of many (often 15-50) CLs need attention since many events on a CL cause it to appear bold in the reviewer's queue even when the event should not logically trigger a new review cycle. For example: * executing automated tests * rebaseing a CL chain * uploading a new patchset that is not yet ready for review This problem leads to either a significant increase in review latency for the team or an additional burden on the most heavily loaded members of a team. Google internal review tools have done an excellent job of solving this problem and its worth reviewing their design and UX. As one possible solution I'd suggest the following simple state engine: * A CL is marked as needs attention when it enters "Incoming reviews" * A CL is unmarked when the reviewer replies * A CL is unmarked when the reviewer hits "Mark reviewed" * A CL is unmarked when the author hits "Not ready for review" * A CL is marked when the author replies * A CL is marked when the author hits "Mark for review" * A CL is marked when the author hits "Mark unreviewed" * Other events do not cause any change in state Using a bold font to indicate which CLs currently need attention feels natural and intuitive.

Most of my reviews for the past 2 weeks have had hours of latency added, because I choose "send" rather than some other harder-to-spot button, and the change remains in WIP status and doesn't get sent to reviewers. As of Gerrit 2.2.2.1, "gerrit review" makes it easy to script review scores and cover letters, but does not have a documented way for scripts to submit inline comments. CBC ciphers are considered vulnerable[1]. It's now recommended practice to prefer CTR ciphers, and some security guidelines even require disabling CBC ciphers[2]. 1. Reduce READ permissions for anonymous users on a project to not include all branches 2. Create a draft patchset in a branch that is READable by anonymous 3. Create a new patchset for the same change, but made publishable 4. Try to git ls-remote over http, it will show the refs for both the draft (/1) and the non draft (/2) 5. fetch the draft over http The draft should not be visible, as the web interface hides it. This is related to issue 1588 , and issue 1589 Currently, if an anonymous user passes the allRefsAreVisible() method on the ProjectControl class then they can see everything. This method only takes into account the READ permission and not draft status. If that function fails, then a VisibleRefFilter class is instantiated to filter out what the user can see. This filter calls that same method, but as it returned false before, it will do the same now. It then finds all the visible changes for that project (taking into account the draft status of the change). This means that to enforce drafts when the anonymous user has READ permissions on the entire project. That is the reason for step 1. The above description is issue 1588 . Note that the VisibleRefFilter only takes into account draft status of a change, not of an individual patch set. A fix to that is to test the patchset as well as the change. I am working on a solution to this, which will be based on issues 1588 and issues 1589 but am happy to submit it to whereever you want it to get feedback.

I tried the described scenario with the "Can merge" feature enabled with both Gerrit 2.5 and current master (aka 2.8) and in both cases I could submit a change which has a pending dependency or which was based on an outdated patch set and the cherry-pick was successfully done. Please note that if you have two open changes A and B, where B depends on A, you can only cherry-pick B if: 1. B does not touch any file that was touched by A 2. All files touched by B that were also touched by A can be automatically merged AND 'Automatically resolve conflicts' is enabled on the project Otherwise it is correct that you get "Can merge: no - requires rebase or has dependency" for B. Can you please check, if in your case B contains files from A and if yes, if the modifications allow an automatic content merge and that 'Automatically resolve conflicts' is enabled on the project?
By the way, clicking on the "Rebase" button may help because in this case Gerrit always tries to do a content merge even if 'Automatically resolve conflicts' is not enabled on the project.

Comment by Shawn Pearce < sop@google.com Actually, I was wrong, MINA SSHD's ChannelOutputStream implements buffering on writes for us, only shifting the buffer to the IO processor when its either full, or an explicit flush was requested.
Update by Shawn Pearce < sop@google.com

Reported by Shawn Pearce < sop@google.com Source: JIRA GERRIT-231 JGit's UploadPack and ReceivePack code does small writes when sending stuff by pkt-line. Basically we do two writes, one for the header length, and one for the payload. These turn into two buffers in MINA SSHD, tripping two interrupts against the IO processor thread, which it then spools out in two write syscalls. This means we are doing 2 write syscalls for every advertised ref in the initial connection advertisement. Its really not efficient use of the server processor. We're better off buffering everything through that phase and then flushing it all at the end mark.
230: pkt-line stuff isn't buffered in sshd

What is the current behavior of that PUT request? If it returns an error, that seems like correct behavior. (If not, we should fix that. :) We could add such an endpoint to check a revision, but the sequence you're describing is inherently racy. Returning an error from the PUT request is not.

It returns an error, as expected. But in this particular use case, issuing the PUT request is not desired as that will actually create the branch when the revision is valid. Branch creations are going to be queued up in an external tool. We want to be able to do some kind of pre-validation before executing them. That includes checking that the given branch point is valid.

The REST API allows to create a branch with: PUT HTTP/1.0 Content-Type: { "revision": " " } Before calling this API, I want to be able to verify that the revision being passed is valid. Something like: GET Note that the revision does not necessarily point to a commit that belongs to a Gerrit change. It could be a commit that has been directly pushed to the repository.

It appears that the patch history table is only showing the number of comments in this patch set, and 0 for any other patch set on this file.

This is actually two different problems. The reason your patch history doesn't show the 24 comments from ps 2 is because the file name changed between the two commits: ps2: 24 comments ps3: 6 comments Gerrit doesn't grok this ps2-ps3 difference well here. Its actually sort of trying to use ps3's file name in order to find the comments from ps2... which fails. I'm working up a patch to try and improve this for some of the common cases, but its downright hard without computing all permutations of patch set against patch set. In this particular example you pointed me to, even Git says the files between ps2 and ps3 are so radically different in content that they aren't a rename. Since there was only one file in each patch set, sure, we could assume they are meant to be a rename and implicitly force that. But that won't hold when there are multiple files in each patch set. So I'm not really sure how to fix this in the general case. And before you say, Git's rename detection sucks and explicit rename is better... what would you have seen in Google Mondrian for this case? The file was new either way (relative to the base branch). The author dropped one path out of the CL in patch set 2 and added a totally different path into patch set 3. IIRC even Mondrian/Perforce wouldn't show this as a rename as there is no place to get the integration data from.

I think change does about as much as we can be expected to do without computing the full permutations of every patch set against every other patch set in the same change just to see if there were renames going on.

Actually, this was a conscious design decision. git-daemon returns the same ambiguous error status when the daemon isn't permitted to read a repository or the repository doesn't exist. This prevents a remote attacker from probing the project namespace to determine if something is hosted here, or not. That said, I can see in certain installations where its OK to leak project names via a probing attack, because users who can access the system are already otherwise restricted to a relatively trusted subset, e.g. a corporate firewall only allows employees to contact the server, or an LDAP directory was configured to only allow users with active LDAP accounts to login to Gerrit. Or the installation is meant to be completely open and an access error is a misconfiguration the administrator wants to identify and resolve. So we should actually make this a configurable option, and default to two different error messages: * "project not found" == name really is not in database * "access denied" == name is correct, user lacks READ +1

Shawn, I can definitely understand the design decision and the security implications. The thought that it was a security decision did cross my mind. I agree with the configurable options you mentioned. Thanks for your help! -Nick

Well, this new configuration option is still not implemented, but at least the error message is documented by now [1] so that users can conclude that they are actually missing read access for the project. [1]

submitWholeTopic does not do precisely what ShaneMcC was asking for. What he wanted (5 years ago) was a gate to hold some commit from being merged if other related topics are unmerged; however, submitWholeTopic is a tool to allow someone to cause all related topics to be submitted with one simple action. Consider I have two changes, A and B, both in the same topic (if that's to be our control mechanism). With submitWholeTopic, I get this feature: When viewing change A, there is a SubmitWholeTopic button. If I press it, A and B are both submitted. What Shane wanted (and I want) is this: When viewing change A, there is a Submit button. If I press it, then if B is already merged = B is "merge pending" = B is not submitted = Quite simply: A is treated as if B is its ancestor. B is treated as if A is its ancestor. One cannot be merged without the other also being merged.

Phil, So here is how I understand the underlying message, correct me if I'm wrong: What you describe are atomic guarantees between A and B. So you actually care about "Either both of A and B are not merged, or both of A and B are merged, there should be no state in which A or B is merged, but not the other." Mind that there is no assertions about SUBMITTED vs NEW. The submitWholeTopic tries to minimize the time of the forbidden state being observable, but doesn't eliminate it. This is because we started to tackle this "two changes are set to the MERGED state atomically" problem in a way which has most impact first. And having minimized the time of the undesired state is doing what you want mostly. Currently I am working on getting rid of the MergeQueue (and eventually the SUBMITTED state), because it doesn't make sense to keep that around. You always had the uncertainty of SUBMITTED, Merge Pending and alike, which is not what the user wants, but the user wants a clear feedback of when a change is not in and when it is. When getting rid of the MergeQueue, we will not send emails any more about failed merges, but rather the UI will tell you if the merge (of both A and B grouped together by the topic) succeeded or not. That way we will minimize the undesired state of one of A,B being merged while the other isn't, a bit further. Eventually later we want to have both A and B to be merged within a single data base transaction.

After that, I only hope Gerrit will do a best-effort attempt at carrying out the merge goals. I take your point about the change-id since they can (now) be re-used on different branches. I actually have an implementation of this "Depends-on" relationship in Jenkins, but there I am on the other side of the fence. When Jenkins wants to test a changeset, I first have it search the "new" patches for any "Depends-on:" directives in the commit log. All the found directives are queried in each submodule of my project using something like this: gerrit query --current-patchset is:open "$@" Any matching commits are fetched and merged, and the newly arriving commits are again searched for new "Depends-on:" directives. (That is, it is recursive.) This continues until no _new_ directives are found. Notice my query searches only for the latest patchset of is:open changes. This means that an abandoned change becomes a non-dependency. This is fine for Jenkins since the point of the dependency system here is to help Jenkins SUCCEED. If there is a real dependency here and it gets ignored, Jenkins will presumably fail during 'make' and the hapless author will need to investigate why. If the query does not match anything, I ignore it. I could complain instead, but for Jenkins' needs, I am willing to fail during the build instead (VRFY:-1 gets people's attention), or to pass if the dependency is already met somehow else. A query may match multiple items in the same and/or different submodules; this is allowed so long as they all merge cleanly. A query which matches the same change-id on multiple branches is likely to cause pandemonium and will fail to merge. This would be a false-negative for Jenkins, but it is an acceptable fallout for such irresponsible construction. In practice we do not have many of these; less than 5% of our changes are ever cherry-picked to another branch, and something less than 1% ever have a "Depends-on" directive; the intersection of these sets is miniscule. But I can see how this could be a problem for Gerrit. I would expect that Gerrit's policy might be to refuse to merge E unless ALL(D) are also merge(able). This would be overly restrictive for some, but it's better than being too permissive. In particular, I don't think you can say E(master) depends on D(master) or similar branch-matching heuristics, because my superproject may lean on different branch names in different projects. Gerrit should not presume to know my wishes. But if it were really a problem, I could do this (though it does not scale): Depends-on:

1. Copy new version to 2. Run bin/gerrit.war init -d $site_path 3. Answer "Y" to copy the WAR file on top of itself. The file is truncated to 0 bytes, and a subsequent restart of the server will fail. If the file we are copying from is the file we are going to copy into, we shouldn't do the copy at all. Better, we should use JGit's LockFile class to perform the update of the WAR, because it won't truncate the destination. Instead it writes to a temporary file, and then renames that on top of the destination if the copy is fully successful.

This describes an ideal world that I think is not the world we live in. Our HTTP serving path has at least 50-100ms of overhead, and, with the ChangeNotes cache, spends like 10ms actually serving the request. The thing about this cache is that that 10ms is roughly constant no matter how many options you request. Considering that each request is 80-90% overhead, making multiple requests is unnecessarily painful for the server. Plus, a browser is only willing to multiplex so many requests at once, so there is a point at which more requests makes things slower. (IIRC Chrome does something like 6 connections per server, but I don't know how that interacts with HTTP/2.) I think we need to at least do some experimentation to see the actual end-user latency impact of 1 request vs. N requests. I agree with your objections to using more ListChangesOptions. However, there are options for how to implement this that do not require adding new options, like returning some fields only from /detail and not in any other path. Well, see above. With NoteDb the cost of filling these out is not as high as you think. It's a lot of copying fields around, granted, but there's no I/O. Good point. Viktar, how are we doing on cache hits for these requests? Maybe there is some low-hanging fruit we are missing in terms of setting cache headers.

I don’t think that all things should be in a single God™ endpoint, but there are opportunities to consolidate some data in circumstances that make sense for the UI. For instance, since messages are below the fold and we have Issue 4752 to hide older messages by default, it doesn’t make sense to include that within the initial change detail request, but there’s other data rendered above the fold that isn’t currently there, and other info that would be useful to include within a response (say, user info or the server config, but I don’t know what the latency ramification are of that so I’d like to wait until Viktar does more investigation). I don’t want to bikeshed on exactly which data is included, but I like that we’re having this discussion and am looking forward to more data.

I did some benchmarking for change and diff view, here are the numbers: Here's the bottleneck: /preferences.diff and changes/xxx/detail finish *after* plugins are loaded and begin doing whatever they want to do - and needless to say, those 2 requests may trigger a diff re-render. So overall, looks like we can shave ~1 sec of user-percepted start time along with jumping/flashing elimination.

Chrome and other HTTP/2 clients use 1 connection for HTTP/2, not 6. And the number of requests the client can send concurrently is dictated by the server. There are two similar but slightly different approaches you could take here. 1. Use the existing /detail endpoint, and nest all the additional information inside ChangeInfo. Similar to how asking for DETAILED_LABELS gets you the labels map, asking for COMMENTS would get you a comments map. All this code lives in ChangeJson.java. Advantages: -Consistent with the current REST API Disadvantages: -ChangeJson is already pretty hairy. -Adding new ListChangesOptions is doable, but we are currently limited to 32 options, and we don't have that many left. (We could also just not use ListChangesOptions, and just always expose these fields from /detail.) This would be my preference, I think. 2. Introduce a new endpoint that basically just sends the concatenation of all the existing requests, i.e. a JSON object containing one ChangeInfo, one map of CommentInfo, one map of FileInfo, etc. Instead of nesting the latter as elements in the ChangeInfo. Advantages: -Doesn't require touching -Could potentially grow into a more flexible way of multiplexing multiple endpoints' worth of requests in a single request. Disadvantages: -Not as consistent with the existing REST API. -Feels weird to be reimplementing HTTP/2 style request multiplexing at the API level.

I agree with dborowitz that implementing multiplexing that should/could happen in HTTP/2 instead is a bad idea in the REST API. Clients can make requests concurrently, those can be handled by different server threads, which may be able to get all data back to the client faster than if it was a single request-response. I'm concerned about adding more options and even more data to ListChangesOption. In someone's infinite wisdom, any plugin that is listening to any change event always gets *all* ListChangesOptions formatting into the ChangeInfo JSON. And we only have a few of these left in the 32-bit space. The GWT UI tried to reduce jank by delaying making the DOM nodes visible until all relevant REST API calls were completed and their data was inserted into the DOM. Once it was all in the DOM, the current node was removed, and the new node had its display:none style removed to make it visible. Some REST API calls like are easily cached by the browser, and may return large amount of data. Isolating these from other calls that return smaller dynamic results makes it easier for the browser to reuse its cache instead of retransferring vast amounts of data from the server.

I don't know that Gerrit is able to distinguish between new commits that aren't yours and existing commits coming from another repository that it may or may not know about. With that in mind, I don't think we can change the error as you said above, but perhaps you have another suggestion?

Am I correct to assume that "git push" won't transfer over the network the objects that are not part of any branch of the target repository but are known (through the alternates setup)? I think I tested this a couple of times and indeed it did not transfer objects over the network that it knew about. Also, Gerrit creates changes (to be reviewed) only for the commits transferred over the network. If so, then why does it complain that I'm not the committer of an object that isn't transferred over the network (and thus no change is made for it) but is known through the alternates file? That's why I thought that the error stems probably from a different check (different than the normal committer check that applies to uploaded commit) and is just the text that is confusing.

Git won't transfer objects from the alternate, because these are advertised to the client as a special ".have" advertisement that isn't visible to Gerrit's receive code. When Gerrit receives the objects, it starts scanning through all commits that are not reachable from the destination branch in this repository. Which will include those commits that are being borrowed from the alternate repository, as they aren't reachable here yet but are still accessible. The only workaround is to create references that point to the alternate repository's same commits. I'll have to think about it more, but it may make sense to consider the tips of the alternate's references to also be not part of what the current user is pushing. Its just not very accessible inside of JGit right now.

Not sure if I fully understood your message but I mean to say that the destination (Gerrit hosted) Git repository had alternates setup, not the client Git repository. "When Gerrit receives the objects, it starts scanning through all commits that are not reachable from the destination branch in this repository." What is "this repository" here? Anyways, if it's complicated to fix then I don't think it's worth it, it's just an error message after all.

Nico worked up this example of what's going on: Local: READ +1 Anonymous users Inherited: READ +1 Anonymous users Inherited: READ +2 Registered users Because everyone is a member of "Anonymous users" group they match that local right of READ +1. This shadows the two inherited READ permissions. The permission system isn't using the inherited permissions here by design. Its done this way so you can do: Local: READ -1 Anonymous users Local: READ +1..+2 Special People Inherited: READ +1 Anonymous users Inherited: READ +2 Registered users This prevents anonymous users from seeing the project, but allows "Special People" to see and upload to it. It can be useful to show most projects, but hide just a select handful. Nico's patch in is going to break this behavior, which man cause surprises for existing installations. Right now, the behavior is "Working as Designed". In my opinion the bug here is that the design is hard to understand, and harder still to diagnose when it doesn't work as expected.

Per this discussion: If a user [accidentally] adds two "Read Access" permissions, in my case for "Anonymous Users" all push access to the project breaks resulting in: % git push gerrit master fatal: Upload denied for project 'news' fatal: The remote end hung up unexpectedly %

Screen reader users need to be able to read diffs in PolyGerrit. Possible ideas: 1. Add role="region" to the row element at the top of each diff chunk, and aria-label="Diff chunk i of n". 2. When pressing arrow keys to navigate, or N and P to move by chunk, put focus on the selected row (tabindex, or Ideally either the table or the row should be focusable 3. For cells that are part of a diff chunk, add some extra text to the grid cell just like a text diff would have - so a "+" or a "-" at the beginning of the line - but then make that character visually hidden so that screen reader users will see it, and it will be copied if you select / copy the text, but not visually shown in the layout. Using CSS clip would be a good way to do that. Please chat with me, dtseng, nektar, & sdoyon to help work out the details.

The polling interval for a change doesn't actually cause it to update, since PG doesn't do any polling of its own. As demonstrated by the bug I just merged into this one, this often leads to confusing situations where people set labels on old patchsets because the page hasn't updated to recognize the newly-uploaded patchset. I'd like to discuss what the fix here would take, or what good workarounds would be if this can't be pulled into -Launch.

My proposal: 1) With or without interval-based polling, it should check for the latest version whenever the reply dialog is opened (or quick review button takes effect). IOW, always check for a newer patch before setting a label. This can be a hard road-block since setting a label on an old PS will fail or be a noop. 2) With that in place for setting labels, the polling feature could be as lightweight as a toast message suggesting a reload. This avoids destructive auto refreshes. Neither (1) nor (2) seem to me as prohibitively heavy for -Launch, but if we just do (1) then it would most-directly deal with the bad reply/label interaction.

Your proposal sounds great to me! (1) seems like a good -Launch bug, since Rietveld didn't have the ability to "vote" on old patchsets, so doing so in PG is a flavor of regression. (2) seems like a good -Afterglow bug, since Rietveld also didn't give you any indication that new patchsets had been uploaded, so PG having toasts is just a nice-to-have.

I agree that it makes not much sense to generate a password in case LDAP is used for authentication. As said login into Gerrit WebUI is handled by LDAP and Git communication is secured by using SSH. So there is not much value in having this generated password. On the other hand in case of LDAP it is annoying that once the user (accidentially) generated a password there is no way back and the password has always to be provided for the Git SSH operations. Are there any reasons against always disabling this button in case of LDAP authentication?
There is a change for review that allows the user to clear the password: With this users can simply remove the password if they have accidentially clicked on the 'Generate Password' button.

Right. So this password exists for HTTP repository access, for URLs like . If you need to login to access the project (or are pushing), we use your username and a password that is dedicated to this repository access purpose. That way its lower risk to embed the password in your ~/.netrc. I put in a clear button in change 15829 as Edwin points out above, but I'm not sure we should actually disable this feature.

So thinking about it further, we don't want to disable the feature altogether, doing so would prevent http:// style of repository access. The better way to do that is to support turning off http:// access altogether, not by hiding the password field in the web UI when LDAP is enabled. So I'm closing this issue as completed since we now have a way for a user to clear the password they accidentally made.

Settings/SSH Keys next to the username/password fields there is a button called Regenerate. When users (accidentally) press that button, a password gets created for the user's SSH account. Once you click it once, the password field in gets set to a new random password. This causes every git SSH operation now prompt for a password, even if things were working fine before clicking the Regenerate button. It's not possible to NULL out the password in the UI. You can only regenerate a new random password, but there's no option to unset it. I think it'd be best to be able to disable this button completely when in LDAP mode. At least I don't see how this feature could provide any value, since authentication for git operations is already done via SSH keys. Not sure if this applies to other authentication methods.
601: Disable (password) Regenerate button in LDAP authentication mode

This is not mere bike-shedding. Re-naming the Reply button to Start Review is a major cause for confusion for users who haven't yet learned this quirk. The fact is that when we did a Usability study all users (IIRC) did not notice that the Reply button had changed text until after a couple of minutes of searching for a way to un-wip the change. So leaving it AS IS would IMO mean ignoring an existing issue.

The current layout/naming is causing confusion. There are 3-4 different issues up on this issue tracker alone related to WIP confusion, so something should probably be done. Changing who can do what hasn't been a cause for confusion as far as I am aware, so I'd just make sure it mirrors current functionality. Show for Change/Project Owners and Admins, Hide for all others. As far as the button location, the reason I chose to add it up there is simply because "Mark as work in progress" is in the "More Actions" dropdown, and it made sense to me to keep them close since they're two sides of the same action. At the same time I wanted Start Review to be immediately accessible, so I didn't want to hide it in the "More Actions" dropdown. I would not be against putting a "Start Review" next to the "Reply" button and have it be blue, since it is arguably the primary action for a change in WIP. The extra warning could be nice, not sure if it is necessary, since the change already has the "Work in Progress" label in the top-left. I believe the state of the change to be fairly clear from our users' perspective, it is mostly the transition between and working in the WIP state that has caused confusion.

On treating owner vs. non-owner: from my interviews with Gerrit users, probably the central use case of WIP is to upload something that is not ready for review yet, so one can look at the diff, run server-side tests, back up the work, etc. without attracting other's attention. All of these reasons seem to imply that it really should be only the owner's discretion to take the change out of WIP as it is currently also only the owner's/author's discretion to upload a change in the first place. So unless there is a good reason for allowing this operation for other users too, I'd vote for removing that action altogether for non-owners. +1 on "Send and Start Review" in the reply dialogue. But again, I'd say this should be visible to the owner only. For consistency, we might then also decide that a newly uploaded change that does not have reviewers yet should have a "set WIP" button as a secondary action outside of the "more" menu.

Hm. Using "git cl patch --force" on the former CL seems to do the trick.
Yep, that's exactly why "git cl patch" has a messaing prompting you to use "--force" if you plan to upload work based on the change you just downloaded.
But why the behavior change? rietveld didn't need this.

Because this is a fundamental feature of how Gerrit works, i.e. with git commit objects instead of textual diffs. The parent of a given commit object must exist on the server, or that commit object is invalid. When you use "git cl patch", it *patches* the commit into your local checkout -- that's effectively a rebase. So the commit which now exists on your machine is *not* the same commit as was uploaded by the owner of the change you patched. So when you then make your own changes based on it and go to upload them, you have a few choices: 1) Upload a new patchset to the other person's change. This is bad: what if they've uploaded real improvements, and then you clobber them with your rebase? 2) Upload a copy of their change as your own. This is also bad: it generates email, duplicates subject lines, confuses attribution, and just clutters things up. 3) Ensure that your commit is based on something that the server already has. This is what "--force" does: it clobbers whatever you have in your local checkout and replaces it with an exact copy of what they uploaded. For a short period of time, "git cl patch" behaved the way it does now with "--force" by default. This made so many people so angry that I had to put it behind a flag and add the warning message instead.

e.g. CL 1, OWNER: oysteine CL 2, OWNER: me In the rietveld world, I would patch down CL 1 using git cl patch. Then I would make a git branch based off of this branch, and then I would set the upstream form the git branch. "git cl upload" would then have the desired effect. In the gerrit world, I get this error when I try to upload my CL: """ Upload upstream branch test47 first. It is likely that this branch has been rebased since its last upload, so you just need to upload it again. """

This has always been true, unfortunately. The TBR= line is only interpreted by PRESUBMIT, not by the code review system or the CQ itself. That's why it is possible to submit changes with lines like "TBR=None" and horrid things like that. The problem here is that you were never actually added as a reviewer, just as a line in the commit message. If you'd been added as a reviewer (or added to the TBR line via git-cl, which would also automatically add you as a reviewer) then it would have worked.

Whatever the technical reason, I think it's really bad that a CL can get merged because of a TBR, without the TBRed person being notified. (If I'm misunderstanding something and this is not actually the case, please correct me.) Issue chromium:500032 does not seem to reflect this problem and also has a very low priority.

Yes, you're 100% correct, it is very very bad. It is also not a change to the status quo -- Rietveld was 100% willing to do this as well. That's the only reason it isn't a higher priority right now. As we rip the Rietveld support code out of the CQ, git-cl, and PRESUBMIT, and switch to supporting a gerrit-only world, addressing this issue will become much easier.
